# **I. What You’ll Get From This**

This guide will walk you through a proven system for building profitable Demand Gen ad campaigns that’s generated 6, 7 figures in revenue with a profitable ROAS for our clients.

**You'll learn how to:**

- Create high-performing creatives across different formats
- Structure campaigns for rapid and efficient scaling
- Build funnels specifically designed for Demand Gen traffic
- Read metrics correctly and optimize campaigns for better ROAS
- And many MORE

**We'll show you how this system works, how we structure it, and how we unlock a channel that most brands write off as “unprofitable”.**

By the end, you'll have a playbook you can apply directly to your own account… whether you're just starting with Demand Gen or looking to scale past seven figures.

# **II. Why Every Ecom Brand Should Test Demand Gen Right Now**

For those who don’t know…

Demand Gen is a campaign type that runs across YouTube (including Shorts), Gmail, Discover and the Google Display Network. Unlike Search or Shopping, the users are consuming content and you have to catch their attention mid scroll (similar to Meta).

!image.png

For some reasons, many people believe that Demand Gen isn’t profitable or it's only for brand awareness.

But that's completely wrong.

Over the past few years, Demand Gen has been one of our most reliable acquisition channels. **It's consistently driven 6, 7 figures in direct revenue for our clients and lifted results across other channels.**

!image.png

*A jewelry brand we took from $3.5k/day to $36k/day spend in 47 days*

!image.png

*A brand we grew from $10k/week to $26k/week spend  in 2 months*

!image.png

*A brand we scaled to $263k/month in spend* 

Now, let's quickly go through what makes Demand Gen so effective in eCom right now.

### **The Scale of the Audience & The Second-Mover Advantage**

Globally, it reaches billions of users every month and captures attention across nearly every age group, income bracket, and intent level.

When a platform has that level of reach, ad budgets naturally follow.

**I personally know a lot of companies that are spending over $10 million/month on Demand Gen alone.**

Google rolled out significant changes to Demand Gen in 2025 and 2026, and the platform has become way more effective.

This creates a rare window where you get the best of both worlds:

- You're competing against fewer advertisers than other channels
- The platform is performing better than it ever has

This year, we’ve tested a ton of different placements, creative formats, and landing pages across the Demand Gen. And we uncovered a lot of strategies that can help brands scale beyond just YouTube Ads (more on that later).

[](data:image/png;base64,)

### **The Halo Effect on Other Channels**

Even though we treat Demand Gen as a direct conversion channel, it's also a powerful brand awareness tool.

With reach campaigns, you can get in front of 1 million people in just 14 days with a A$10,000 budget. That makes it an incredible tool for sales events or retargeting warm audiences.

**We've also found that Demand Gen contributes significantly to indirect conversions.**

Many people watch a Demand Gen ad (YouTube in this case), get interested, but don't convert immediately. Instead, they go search for the brand on Google, check it out on Amazon, or visit the Shopify store later.

!image.png

[](data:image/png;base64,)

*Google Ads reported a 1.4 ROAS. The Impact on Shopify was 3.75 ROAS. But when we factored in Shopify and Amazon, the actual ROAS was 5.25.*

When someone sees your Demand Gen, they rarely convert immediately. Instead, they might:

- Google your brand name later
- Visit your website through social media
- Buy on Amazon after seeing your product in a store
- Purchase weeks later when they finally need your solution

And the best thing about Demand Gen is:

**Very few brands take it seriously.**

This leaves a wide open lane for smart brands to exploit. Today, we're going to show you exactly how to tap into this opportunity.

# **III. The Top-Performing Demand Gen formats in 2026**

In this section, we cover the formats every ecom brand should be testing in 2026. We'll keep it high-level here and go deeper on the creatives in the next section.

From our tests, there are 4 formats that consistently perform well for our clients

## A. YouTube In-stream Ads

Video ads that play before or during YouTube videos

!YouTube Watch Page (Desktop)_EN-1.svg_EN-1.svg)

**The in-stream user** clicked on a video and got interrupted. They know it's an ad. They're in long-form mode.

**Average watch times**: 20+ seconds

How to think about this format:

- The video does all the heavy lifting. There’s no supporting copy or images.
- Use the first 5 seconds to define your audience with a direct problem callout.
- Give a compelling reason to leave YouTube right now (limited time, exclusive offer).
- Shoot in landscape format.

## B. YouTube Short Ads

Shown in between vertical Shorts content as users scroll through the Shorts feed on mobile.

!image.png

**The Shorts user** is scrolling for quick dopamine. Whether or not it registers as an ad barely matters if it's entertaining.

**Average watch times**: around 8 seconds

How to think about this format:

- Users often do not register it as an ad, so lean into organic content.
- Your hook needs to land in one second.
- UGC, first-person, funny, and relatable content wins here.
- The bottom of Shorts is covered by UI elements (like, comment, and share buttons plus a description), so keep key visuals and copy higher in the frame.
- Copy carries more influence here than it does in in-stream.
- Make it something genuinely entertaining to watch.

## C. YouTube In-feed Ads

Appear across the YouTube homepage, search results, and alongside suggested videos while users browse.

!image.png

**The in-feed user** is browsing thumbnails looking for something to watch. An image caught their eye.

How to think about this format:

- This is thumbnail advertising, not video advertising.
- There is no audio unless a user actively chooses to watch, which happens less than 1% of the time.
- Your creative (video or image) has to grab attention on its own.
- Long copy is visible and supports the creative
- You are competing directly with YouTube's organic thumbnail ecosystem, so match the dopamine-seeking intent of someone actively browsing.

## D. Google Discover

Google’s content feed that shows up when someone opens the Google app or lands on a new tab in Chrome mobile.

!image.png

How to think about this format:

- This is a native feed placement, your creative needs to blend in with organic content. Don’t make it feel too promotional.
- You're reaching people who weren't looking for you, and you have to catch their attention mid scroll with the images and headlines

# IV. Campaign Setup & Strategy

## 1. Campaign Structure

We've looked at hundreds of Demand Gen ad accounts, and we're seeing the same pattern over and over:

**The best results come from more campaigns.** But that doesn't mean messy duplications or cluttered accounts.

These are deliberate separations by creative, audience, and funnel intent.

### **What Goes Wrong in Messy Accounts**

When everything is crammed into one campaign:

- The system favors 1-2 creatives and ignores the rest
- Audiences get mixed together with no clear learning

### **The Clean Structure That Works**

Build lean, focused campaigns with:

- **1-2 creatives per campaign**
- **Clear audience targeting**
- **Matching landing pages**

**Example structure:**

- **Campaign A:** "Bloating relief" hook → Health-focused audience → Soft CTA landing page
- **Campaign B:** "Weight loss" hook → Fitness audience → Direct offer page
- **Campaign C:** "Energy boost" hook → Busy professionals → Quiz funnel

(I’ll talk more about landing pages later.)

We also build separate campaigns for different formats.

It’s generally better to test multiple formats but you don’t need all different formats to start. We’ve seen brands go from 0 to $80k/mo spend within 40 days running only in-stream ads.

Whatever format you choose, the biggest lever is always the creatives.

### **Why This Structure Wins**

The goal is to learn quickly and scale with clarity.

When your data is clean, you can:

- See which angles are working
- Kill what's not performing
- Double down with certainty

This also protects creatives from burnout. One strong video can collapse in performance if it's forced to carry too many jobs.

**We've seen 10-20% lifts in ROAS in just 2-3 weeks by unstacking messy campaigns and giving each message its own runway.**

## 2. Targeting

### **For New Customers**

Use broad but relevant targeting:

- Broad demographics (age, gender, location)
- Lookalike audiences based on your best customers
- Custom intent audiences (built from keyword, topic clusters, or competitor terms)
- Interest groups based on search queries

**Make sure to exclude past purchasers, site visitors, AND your email lists.**

Otherwise, your "cold" Demand Gen campaign will quietly run as a remarketing campaign.

*(I know some agencies do it on purpose to show better conversion data. Be careful.)*

### **For Remarketing**

Segment users by their engagement level:

- Video viewers (people who watched your ads)
- Site visitors (people who visited but didn't buy)
- Cart abandoners (people who started checkout)

Then match the message to their level of awareness. Someone who watched 75% of your ad needs a different message than someone who abandoned their cart.

### **Using Seed Audiences**

Leverage seed audiences like customer lists or engaged website users. This lets Google find similar buyers and speeds up optimization.

## **3. Competitor Research**

Creatives are the most important piece of Demand Gen ads.

We have seen brands with suboptimal setup and broad targeting crush it purely because they have strong ads.

The creative process usually starts with in-depth customer research (customer reviews, support tickets, and forums) to find what resonates with the audience. Then we study what ads are already working in the market using three tools:

### **Tool #1: VidTao**

**What you can do:**

- Filter by industry, spending levels, and duration
- Download actual creative files (not just screenshots)
- See traffic data showing how much brands are spending
- Identify ads that have run for 90+ days (a signal that they're profitable and scaling)

### **Tool #2: Panoramata**

While VidTao helps you find creative ideas, Panoramata lets you watch the exact moves specific brands are making across their entire marketing ecosystem.

**Here's what Panoramata tracks:**

- Email campaigns (what competitors are sending to their lists)
- Website changes (when they update landing pages or offers)
- Social media content (their organic content strategy)
- Ad campaigns across all platforms, including YouTube
- You can view YouTube ads directly in the platform itself

### **Tool #3: Google's Transparency Center**

**What you can view:**

- All their current ads
- Basic targeting information (filter by countries)
- Direct links to existing video creatives
- When ads were last shown

**The limitations:**

- It only shows currently running ads, not historical data
- You don't see the landing page used with the video ad (which is crucial)

### Here's a summary of each tool:

!image.png

For the winning creatives, we pay the most attention the to hook structure, emphasized benefits, CTAs, video length, and format.

## **4. Creatives**

### **Video Ads**

Video is the backbone of most Demand Gen strategies. As a general rule of thumb, the creative should be divided into 3 main parts:

- Hook:
    - A pattern interrupt (an unexpected sound or image)
    - A contrarian statement ("Stop drinking 8 glasses of water a day")
    - A huge pain point ("Cutting your dog's nails this way causes trauma")
- Body:
    - Once you have their attention, give them something useful immediately. If they feel smarter or validated in the first few seconds, they are far more likely to stick around.
        - Teach them a quick tip
        - Explain the real reason behind their problem
        - Call out a myth your industry keeps repeating
        - Show a fast satisfying result.
        - Before-and-after style content works exceptionally well here.
    - Then introduce your product, stack value props, address common objections
- CTA: Say it out loud, tell them to click, and reinforce it with an on-screen overlay.

#### 5 video formats that are working right now:

- **VSL:** a credible voice walks through the problem like they're having a conversation with the viewer.
- **UGC:** A person talking about your product in an authentic, unpolished way.
- **Animation Ads:** Illustrated or motion graphics-based ads that simplify a complex product or concept. Good for explaining a mechanism.
- **Podcast-Style Influencer Ad**: framed as a two-person conversation. The host asks questions related to the product/problem, the influencer answers.
- **Stitched Clip Ad (4-6 mins):** A longer format built by combining multiple content clips or ads into one cohesive video.

### Image Ads

When it comes to Demand Gen, it seems like the only format people talk about is YouTube video ads.

But if that's all you're running… you'll be missing out on other formats that can drive meaningful revenue for your brand.

One that's been working really well for our clients recently is **image ads**.

I go through hundreds of campaigns weekly and every time I look, I see more of these crushing it in our accounts. A lot of them are pulling over **2X ROAS** on cold traffic.

One client generated **$152K** using this format at a **2.45X ROAS**.

!image.png

Another made **$50K** at a **2.2X ROAS** within 2 months after launch.

!image.png

---

Image Ads on Demand Gen appear in:

- **In-Feed YouTube Ads**
- **Google Discover**

A lot of brands write these off as junk placements and exclude them by default. But the issue is usually the ads, not the placements.

What works here is getting intentional with the **angle** behind each ad and building the image, the copy, and the landing page around that angle together.

#### 3 Angles That Consistently Perform

1. **Offer-Focused**

For this angle, the image exists to communicate a deal, and everything in the creative is built around that.

This angle works when you're targeting an audience that already knows your product or category and just needs a reason to act.

**There are 2 versions of this format worth knowing:**

**Single Product Offer Image**

The product sits on a clean background, centered, with clear space for the offer headline.

The copy in the image does the heavy lifting with a bold offer and a short urgency line underneath.

!image.png

**Multi-Panel Collection Layout**
This works well when you want to showcase multiple products from a collection, different items in a range, or several colorways of the same product within a single frame.

!image.png

---

**2. Informational Angle**

This angle is built around a problem your audience is dealing with or an outcome they're actively trying to reach.

The ad leads with the problem, framed in a way that makes the viewer feel like they're about to learn something useful.

A headline in this format might look like:

> *"Doctors Explain the Hidden Reason Why Most Knee Pain Treatments Don't Work"*
> 

!image.png

For the image, the goal is to add curiosity to that angle rather than give the answer away.

A few directions that work well:

- A person visibly experiencing the problem in a relatable, everyday context
- A split scene showing the before and after states
- A close-up of the affected area or body part, shot in a way that feels clinical or editorial. Works well for health and wellness products.

---

**3. Comparison Angle**

This angle takes multiple products in your category and ranks them, positioning your ad as a trusted recommendation rather than a promotion.

A headline here might look like:

> *"Physical Therapists Are Calling These 6 Massagers a Game-Changer for Mobility After 40"*
> 

!image.png

For the image, the goal is to add curiosity and draw the viewer in rather than revealing the conclusion. A few directions that work well:

- A clean editorial flat lay of the products from the category
- A lifestyle scene where a person is using one of the products in a natural context, with subtle visual cues that suggest comparison or research. Like a notepad nearby or multiple products visible in the background.
- A split-panel layout showing two products side by side with a clear visual hierarchy that implies one is the preferred choice, without explicitly saying so in the image.

## **5. Funnels**

**Funnel #1:** Video Ads/Image Ads → Quiz Funnels

!video to quiz.png

**Funnel #2:** Video Ads → VSL Pages

!Group 119 (2).png.png)

**Funnel #3**: Offer-focused Image/Video Ads → Dedicated product page or collection page about the sale

!Group 2330.png

**Funnel #4**: Informational Image Ads → Advertorials

!image.png

**Funnel #5**: Comparison Image Ads → 3rd-party Comparison Page

!image.png

## 6. Scaling & Optimization Strategy

Every week, pull performance data by:

- View-through rate (where does attention drop)
- Audience segments (which specific groups convert)
- Placement type
- Device (mobile vs. desktop vs. TV screens)

Identify the combinations that are working and redirect budget toward those pockets.

Remove or reduce spend on audiences performing below your target ROAS, pause creatives that are not pulling their weight after reasonable spend, and exclude placements and devices that are not converting efficiently.

We have worked with brands that pushed 50-plus video assets, but only 3 became consistent performers. And one drove most of the revenue.

**To scale month over month, build a system to test new creatives, the same way you would on Meta.**

Set up a dedicated testing campaign for new formats, feed performance insights back into creative production, and move winning videos into scaling campaigns.

A rule of thumb for scaling budgets:

Increase by 20% on your winners every 3 to 5 days if performance holds.

<aside>
⚠️

Google’s reported ROAS usually understates actual performance, especially on prospecting.

Google might show a 1.0x ROAS while TripleWhale shows 1.3x after cross-channel attribution gets factored in.

**Take that into account alongside your LTV when making scaling decisions.**

</aside>

## 7. Interpreting Performance Data

This is a tricky subject that we’ve seen a lot of brands mess up.

First, let’s talk about how to know if Demand Gen are truly incremental or not.

### 3 levers that determine whether your Demand Gen campaigns are driving new revenue

**1. Who You're Actually Reaching**

Even with interest targeting set and broad reach intended, Smart Bidding will always find the path of least resistance. It goes after existing customers first, then retargeting audiences, then cold prospects.

As I mentioned in the targeting section:

*If you are not actively excluding past purchasers, site visitors, and your email lists, your "cold" YouTube campaign is quietly running as a remarketing campaign.*

**2. Your Business Model**

The nature of your product determines how aggressively you need to exclude.

For a one-time-purchase brand, showing Demand Gen ads to existing buyers is nearly useless.

For supplement brands, subscription businesses, or consumables, repeat buyers will come back regardless… claiming that conversion as a win can be dangerous.

**3. Your Conversion Window**

By default, Google counts engaged view conversions (someone watches 10-plus seconds of your video) within a 3-day window as a full conversion, weighted identically to an actual click.

A lot of agencies quietly extend this to 30 days to make campaign performance look strong.

Someone watches your ad for 10 seconds, does not click, then buys 3 weeks later through a completely different channel, and your YouTube campaign gets 100% of the credit.

**Here is how to check your numbers:**

Go to Segment, then Conversions, then Ad Event Type.

Look at what percentage of your conversions come from clicks versus engaged views.

- If it is 90%+ clicks, you have high confidence the ad actually did the work.
- If it is 20% or more engaged views, your numbers are inflated.

### How to know if Demand Gen are truly incremental or not.

**Mixed Model Attribution is the standard for measuring this accurately, but it can be expensive to set up.**

So here are 2 free, simple ways to do it.

*They won’t give you the full picture on the impact of YouTube Ads, but they’ll give you signals to know whether it’s driving new customers to your store.*

**Method 1: Branded Search Correlation**

Go to Google Search Console => Performance tab => filter by your exact brand name. Look at impressions day by day for the last 90 days. 

Now overlay when your Demand Gen campaigns were active vs paused.

If branded search impressions spike when Demand Gen is running and drop when it's off => Demand Gen is creating demand.

You can literally see the correlation in the data. Cross-reference this with your Shopify conversion dates.

If branded search goes up and conversions follow 1-3 days later => Demand Gen is driving sales, just not getting credit.

**Method 2: Native Tracking Comparison**

Use Google Ads platform data for this.

Check your Demand Gen campaign ROAS (let's say 1.2).

Now check your Search campaign ROAS (also showing 1.2) → Search is bottom-funnel, just fulfilling existing demand.

If Search and YouTube show similar ROAS in-platform, YouTube is actually contributing WAY more because it's creating the demand that Search and other channels are capturing.

Look at your Search terms report. If you see branded search volume increasing when YouTube is active → YouTube is feeding Search.

# Takeaway

It surprises us how many e-commerce brands either ignore Demand Gen entirely or abandon it after looking at in-platform metrics.

They're leaving massive revenue on the table.

Treat YouTube as a unique platform and give it the same attention as your Meta campaigns. Most brands have not cracked Demand Gen yet. Stick with it and you will quickly scale to $100k+ per month while competitors are complaining about the next Meta algorithm update.
